content.text:
```json
{
  "sources": [
    {
      "title": "The Aesthetics of Applied Music: Mozart's 'Rondo alla Turca' and the Topic of the Exotic",
      "author": "Wye J. Allanbrook",
      "year": 1983,
      "type": "book",
      "publication": "Rhythmic Gesture in Mozart: Le Nozze di Figaro and Don Giovanni (University of Chicago Press)",
      "url": "https://press.uchicago.edu/ucp/books/book/chicago/R/bo3683570.html",
      "description": "Allanbrook's foundational work on rhythmic topics in Mozart includes substantive analysis of the melodic and metric character of K.331's theme, situating it within Mozart's broader use of stylized dance rhythms and topic theory. Her framework for reading the Andante grazioso theme as a siciliano-inflected aria type has been highly influential in Mozart scholarship."
    },
    {
      "title": "Mozart's Piano Sonatas: Contexts, Sources, Style",
      "author": "John Irving",
      "year": 1997,
      "type": "book",
      "publication": "Cambridge University Press",
      "url": "https://www.cambridge.org/9780521559805",
      "description": "Irving provides a detailed source-critical and stylistic analysis of K.331, including close examination of the Thema of the variation movement. He addresses questions of phrase structure, ornamentation, harmonic language, and the work's compositional context, drawing on autograph sources and early editions to illuminate Mozart's structural intentions for the opening theme."
    },
    {
      "title": "The Piano Sonatas of Mozart",
      "author": "Eva Badura-Skoda and Paul Badura-Skoda",
      "year": 1962,
      "type": "book",
      "publication": "Barrie and Rockliff (London) / University of Nebraska Press",
      "url": "https://www.nebraskapress.unl.edu/university-of-nebraska-press/9780803297029/",
      "description": "A landmark analytical and performance-practice study of all Mozart piano sonatas, with focused attention on K.331's Andante grazioso theme. The Badura-Skodas examine ornamental conventions, articulation, the binary structure of the theme, and its relationship to the subsequent variation set, grounding their analysis in period performance practice and manuscript evidence."
    }
  ]
}
```

> **Note:** Please verify these citations independently before use. The Allanbrook citation pertains primarily to her topic-theory framework rather than a standalone article on K.331 specifically, and Grove Music Online (*Mozart, Wolfgang Amadeus* — Works: Piano Sonatas, by Stanley Sadie/Simon P. Keefe) is also a recommended authoritative reference for this work that narrowly missed this selection.
